# `sesam rm` stops tracking a secret: it records a secret.remove entry, drops the
# entry from the config and prunes the sealed object. --force also deletes the
# revealed plaintext, plain rm leaves it in place.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# Track two secrets.
exec sesam add keep.txt --group dev
exec sesam add drop.txt --group dev
exists .sesam/objects/drop.txt.sesam
exec sesam ls
stdout 'drop.txt'

# Remove one: audit records secret.remove, it leaves the listing and config, and
# the sealed object is pruned.
exec sesam rm drop.txt
exec sesam show .sesam/audit/log.jsonl
stdout '"secret.remove"'
exec sesam ls
! stdout 'drop.txt'
stdout 'keep.txt'
! exists .sesam/objects/drop.txt.sesam
! grep 'drop.txt' sesam.yml
exec sesam verify

# Without --force the revealed plaintext stays on disk.
exists drop.txt

# --force also deletes the revealed plaintext.
exec sesam rm keep.txt --force
! exists keep.txt
exec sesam verify

-- keep.txt --
keep me
-- drop.txt --
drop me
